observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations and management agreed that:

The Municipal Treasurer (i) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (ii) instruct the liquidating officer to be more circumspect in the acknowledgement of remittances in terms of accuracy of the data and amount; (iii) instruct all the collecting officers to (1) ensure completeness of information on the face of official receipts and RCD; (2) ensure that each official receipts are properly signed or initialed; (3) properly accomplish the Box C of the RCD; and (4) prepare and submit the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF); and (iv) prepare and submit the monthly Consolidated Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) pursuant to Section 37 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992; and






The Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of the recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the errors in computations, as appropriate.
observations.managementAction:
Remittances by the collecting officers to the Municipal Treasurer has improved thus the noted longest delay was only seven days.

The Municipal Treasurer checked the correctness of the data and amount before acknowledging the remittances. 

The Municipal Treasurer checked the completeness of information on the face of official receipts and RCD; ensured that the official receipts are duly signed or initialed; made sure that Box C of the RCD were accomplished; and the monthly RAAF was prepared and submitted by the collecting officers.

The Municipal Treasurer prepared and submitted the monthly CRAAF and it was being submitted per in CY 2021 due to travel restrictions brought about by COVID-19 pandemic. 

The Municipal Accountant adjusted the balances of the accounts affected by the errors noted by the audit team.
observations.reason: Remittances of collecting officers were still delayed and deposit of collections were still not intact.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor (a) require strict and uniform implementation of the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to all officials and employees of the Municipality; and (b) require all accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate their outstanding cash advances by sending confirmation, notification or demand letters.


For unliquidated cash advances which are non-moving for more than three years, exhaust all remedies to collect or demand to liquidate the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, apply for write off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016 which prescribes the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs).

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that, as the internal auditor, (a) see to it that certification for expenses not requiring receipt for expenses amounting to P300.00 and below is attached to the liquidation/reimbursement of travel pursuant to COA Circular No. 2017-001 dated June 19, 2017; and 


(b) reclassify the balance of Advances for Payroll and Advances to Officers and Employees pertaining to the cash advances of accountable officers who were already not in the service, retired or dead totaling P108,182.69.
observations.managementAction:
The municipality now strengthens their internal control on the grant and liquidation of cash advances. 

The Municipal Accountant has sent demand letters to delinquent officers and employees. For instance, demand letter dated June 10, 2021 was sent to the Municipal Nutrition Action Officer for the unliquidated amount of P3,375.00.




The Municipal accountant required the attachments of certification for expenses not requiring receipt for expenses amounting to P300.00 and itwas attached to the liquidation or reimbursement of travel.

The Municipal Accountant drew adjusting entries to reclass to Other Receivable account the non-moving unliquidated cash advances of the accountable persons already separated from the municipality.
observations.reason:
Unliquidated cash advances as at year-end and late liquidations were still noted by the audit team.



Still, no demand was made for the non-moving cash advances to facilitate its liquidation and settlement in CY 2021. Also, no request for authority to write-off was submitted to the audit team.

observations.managementAction:
The QRF and the list of PPAs charged to the special trust fund were already incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.



The Municipal Accountant has drawn adjustments for the noted accounting errors in the recording of receipt of donations and in the utilization of LDRRMF. Likewise, proper entry was also recorded for the subsequent and similar transactions.

The management has already submitted trip tickets and warranty security. Moreover, the BAC already ensures completeness of supporting papers for the procurement of goods, services and infrastructure projects. No procurement of motor vehicle was made in CY 2021.

The pictures of billboards showing the vital information that should be disclosed to the public were already being attached to the vouchers.
observations.reason: However, misclassification as to whether capital outlay or MOOE was still noted in the LDRRMFIP and AIP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG to ensure strict compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements.  
Also, submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated.  

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer agreed that the pictures attached to the payments of infrastructure projects includes/covers the embedded items in accordance with Item No. 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC submitted the noted lacking Bid Evaluation Report of the projects. Moreover, the BAC has been exerting full effort to comply with the procurement guidelines.



The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer already ensured that the pictures of embedded items are attached as supporting documents for the progress billing of contracts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that the:

Municipal Development Council, in the preparation of Investment Program, make sure that the programs, projects and activities identified are specific and in accordance with the requirements of the existing guidelines covering that fund;

BAC and its members be guided by the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement of goods and services and be always abreast with the issuances of the GPPB so that various requirements thereto are always complied with; and


Municipal Accountant (i) be guided by Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 in the documentation of transactions; (ii) properly record donations received by the Municipality in accordance with COA Circular 2014-002; and (iii) disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the procurement, issuance and inventory of goods and property, plant and equipment procured by the Municipality including those acquired through donations either in cash or in-kind.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Development Council has already itemized the PPAs in the preparation of the AIP.


The BAC and its members have exerted their full effort to comply with the procurement law for the purchase of goods and services.


The Municipal Accountant already ensures that the vouchers were supported with complete documentation before processing of payments. And the donations received were already recorded to its proper accounts.
observations.reason: As to the required disclosure, no such disclosure was made in the Notes to FS for the procurement, issuance, and inventory of goods and property, plant and equipment procured by the municipality under the LDRRMF including those acquired through donations in cash or in-kind.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Municipal Mayor instruct the:

The Municipal Accountant and the concerned employee who prepares the payroll to strictly observe the provisions of LBC No. 103.  The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, applications for leave of the department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due; and

Municipal Accountant to make sure that required supporting documents of payrolls are complete pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445. 


We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the refund of the net excess payments of RATA amounting to P39,750.00 to officials and employees of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The payrolls for the payment of RATA as computed by the HRMO were already guided and supported with daily time records, application for leave, and attendance sheet of SB Members 


The payrolls for the payment of RATA were supported with DTRs, leave application, and attendance sheet of SB members during sessions.


The management, through the Municipal Accountant, already collected the noted excess payments of RATA from the responsible officials with total amount of P39,750.00.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Mayor along with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Engineer strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% Development Fund and their implementation.  Ensure that these programs/projects/activities partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality.  See to it also that those development projects identified in the AIP are fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents; and


The Municipal Accountant (i) be guided by the requirements of Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160 for proper documentation of claims against government funds; and (ii) submit the lacking documents mentioned above.
observations.managementAction:
The PPAs in CY 2021 identified in the fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality.




The Municipal Accountant already ensures that the claims against government funds are supported with complete documentation.
observations.reason:
Five PPAs identified in CY 2020 were still not yet implemented in CY 2021.




However, the pictures showing the embedded items and the billboard or signboard as noted in the findings were still not submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through the GAD focal point system (i) synchronize the preparation of GAD Plan and Budget with the planning and budgeting calendar to enable its timely submission to the PPDO to check its alignment with the priorities of the Province and then to the DILG Provincial office for review and endorsement; (ii) develop PPAs that would address gender issues in the Municipality to strengthen its gender-responsive governance; and (iii) ensure that GAD Fund is fully utilized efficiently and effectively in compliance with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 as amended.
observations.reason: The GAD Plan and Budget was not submitted to the PPDO on time due to oversight of the concerned municipal officials.
Same lapses and deficiencies were noted in the audit of CY 2020 GAD Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed that the:
BAC (i) submits all the original/authenticated copies of the lacking supporting documents and proper justifications for the non-compliance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other COVID-19 related issuances on the procurement of  infrastructure projects to arrive at a decision in audit;                  (ii) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of the said 2016 Revised IRR to establish the validity and regularity of the procurement process and award of contracts/POs and to ensure that information on the funds and their use are reliable, accurate and transparent to establish accountability in the procurement process; and
Head of the Accounting Office checks the completeness and propriety of supporting documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and other COVID-19 related issuances before processing claims for payments to establish the legality, validity and propriety of the disbursements.

We recommended and Management committed that the:
Municipal Treasurer and concerned offices institute appropriate collection procedures to recover the non-moving dormant accounts and to ensure cash availability for future beneficiaries of various loan programs; and
Head of the Accounting Office conducts regular and periodic verification of loan receivable accounts to determine its conditions that would affect its collectability.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to cause the prompt implementation of priority PPAs funded out of grants from various NGAs and require the:
Concerned municipal officials and employees, particularly the Municipal Agriculturist, OIC-Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer and Municipal Health Officer, to (i) promptly resolve issues in the delayed project implementation/completion through submission of complete listing of qualified fisherfolks by the Fishery Chairperson, among others; and (ii) facilitate the preparation and submission of project proposals to utilize funds from the DA, BFAR, DSWD, NNC and PhilHealth for the Municipality and the local residents to enjoy the immediate use and benefits that could be obtained therefrom; and
Head of the Accounting Office to (i) prepare and submit the FURs to DA and DSWD pertaining to the various livelihood programs released as loans to beneficiaries; and (ii) refund the unexpended balance to the concerned agencies or to the BTr to clean the Due to NGAS account. And for fair and accurate presentation of the account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the Chairperson of the MDRRM Council cause for the optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund to strengthen the resilience of the constituents to disasters, climate change impacts and the threats brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
MDRRM Officer to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP indicating the detailed PPAs and the corresponding amounts of appropriations for each PPAs;
Head of the Accounting Office to (i) reclassify the financial assistance from the BTr from the Due to NGAs account to the Trust Liability-DRRM account for the correct presentation of the account balances in the financial statements; and (ii) check the completeness and propriety of the supporting documents before processing claims for payments; and
Municipal Treasurer to issue PAR for the generator sets and motorcycles to establish the accountability thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effectively cause the optimal utilization of the 20% Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to the local constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to revisit the PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by 2016-01, to (i) synchronize the preparation of GPB and GAD AR and ensure its timely submission to the PPDO and to the DILG Provincial Office, to check its alignment with the priorities of the Province for review and endorsement, respectively; (ii) ensure that the 5% GAD budget is fully appropriated into GAD-related PPAs in the formulation of GPB; and (iii) develop PPAs that would address gender issues in the Municipality to strengthen its gender-responsive governance and enable integration of GAD-related activities in its regular operation.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts.  The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct physical count on all inventories on hand periodically which should be reported in the RPCI and shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and (ii) complete the preparation of RPCPPE on the physical count of PPE accounts and submit same to the Audit Team;

instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to fast track the reconciliation of the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records.  In the event that deficiencies/ discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made; and

require the Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which could be undertaken simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to (a) exert extra efforts to account the unreconciled items of two PNB accounts appearing in the BRS of the LBP accounts to faithfully present the correct balances of the accounts; (b) prepare the necessary adjusting entry to record the unrecorded amount in the Cash in Bank account; and (c) prepare and submit the BRS together with the bank statements, paid checks and debit/credit memos for all the Municipality’s bank accounts within the reglementary period.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor remind the managing head of the depository bank of the Municipality of their duty and responsibility to furnish the Local Accountant with the Bank Statements (BS) including debit/credit memos (DM/CM), paid checks, etc., within five days after the end of each month pursuant to Item 3.1 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the overdue accounts and render report on the status of the demand letters sent to enforce collection thereof to protect the interest of the government.  
   
We also recommended that local officials expedite the submission of the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs) to the Commission on Audit in line with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, stating the reasons for write-off and extent of follow-up made.  The management should see to it that the request for write - off is duly supported with the documents required in Item 8.3 of the mentioned circular.

We recommended that:

The Municipal Treasurer (i) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (ii) instruct the liquidating officer to be more circumspect in the acknowledgement of remittances in terms of accuracy of the data and amount; (iii) instruct all the collecting officers to (1) ensure completeness of information on the face of official receipts and RCD; (2) ensure that each official receipts are properly signed or initialed; (3) properly accomplish the Box C of the RCD; and (4) prepare and submit the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF); (iv) ensure that all the issued and cancelled official receipts are attached to their respective Report of Collections and Deposits; and (v) prepare and submit the monthly Consolidated Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) pursuant to Section 37 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992; and

The Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of the recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the errors in computations, as appropriate.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor (a) require strict and uniform implementation of the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to all officials and employees of the municipality; (b) require all accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate all their outstanding cash advances by sending confirmation, notification or demand letters.  For unliquidated cash advances which are non-moving for more than three years, exhaust all remedies to collect or demand to liquidate the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise apply for write off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016 which prescribes the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs); and (c) secure written approval from the Governor for any official travel outside the province in accordance with Section 4 of EO No. 77.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, (a) see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers and liquidation reports are accurate and complete before processing the claims and observe the existing rules and regulations; and (b) reclassify the balance of Advances for Payroll and Advances to Officers and Employees pertaining to the cash advances of accountable officers who were already not in the service, retired or dead totaling P108,182.69.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to ensure proper compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements.  Meanwhile, cause the investigation on the procurement procedures adopted by the BAC on the subject 12 projects and hold accountable those found remiss of their respective functions and duties.  Also, submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated.  Moreover, furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution thereof as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009.  

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are valid, proper and legal and supported with complete documentation before processing the claims in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160.  Submit also the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  instruct the BAC to: (a) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five working days from the execution of the contract as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; (b) make sure that Purchase Orders contain basic data/information as prescribed by COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996; (c) observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; (d) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post-Qualification Evaluation Reports; (e) submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated; and (f) prepare various reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors which could mislead the readers/users of the reports.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, (a) see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are complete before processing the claims in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b) be guided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 for proper recording of transactions in accordance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS).

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:

Municipal Accountant and the concerned employee who prepares the payroll to strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 103.  The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, applications for leave of the department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due;

Municipal Accountant (i) make sure that required supporting documents of payrolls are complete pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445; and (ii) take extra care in recording of transactions in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and

Human Resource Officer to scrutinize the Applications for Leave of each officials and employees and make sure that they are properly filled up to avoid confusion as to the type of leave.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the refund of excess payments of RATA amounting to P90,750.00 to officials and employees of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Municipal Local School Board (LSB) to: (i) adhere to the provisions of Section 272 of R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 in planning the programs, projects and activities (PPAs) to be charged to the fund and its proper utilization; (ii) review and approve the SEF budget for the ensuing year on the prescribed schedule as stated in the said joint circular; and (iii) make sure that PPAs included in the budget are properly supported with details of the PPAs to avoid charging of expenses which are not related to the purpose of the fund; and

instruct the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, to see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers/liquidation reports are complete before processing the claim in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2017-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the approving officials to refrain from paying terminal leave benefits of officials and employees who are not yet resigned, retired or separated from service.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the legality and propriety of the payments made as well as the completeness of supporting documents.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, MSWDO and the MAO to exert their best effort to collect long-outstanding receivables by (i) continuously sending collection notice to concerned debitors; and (ii) instilling in the minds of future beneficiaries the need to return the amount to ensure fund availability and continuity of the program.
observations.managementAction: The Management continuously sends demand letters to the delinquent borrowers, thus, the collection efficiency increased from 2% in CY 2019 to 12.52% in CY 2020.
observations.reason: Low family income of some borrowers, while for some no earnings due to work suspensions brought about by COVID-19. This observation is discussed in the Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (a) BAC and the Municipal Engineer to promptly implement the projects in accordance with the specific purpose of the trust fund for the constituents to enjoy the benefits of the government’s programs and projects at the soonest time;        (b) Municipal Agricultural Officer to coordinate with the Fishery Chairman of Looc for the prompt submission of the list of qualified fisher folks to facilitate the release/distribution of incentives amounting to P1,669,521.80; and (c) Municipal Treasurer to remit the unused balance of Due to NGAs account to the concerned source agencies.
observations.managementAction:
The Bottoms-Up Budgeting project from the DILG for Construction of Water Supply System Level II in various barangays were fully implemented during the year. While the unutilized amount of 
P5,656,744.25 from the DENR for National Greening Program was fully refunded during the year.
observations.reason: The Management cited the lack of interested contractors/ suppliers as one of the reasons for the delayed and/or non-implementation of projects/programs.

observations.managementAction: The APP was prepared and submitted in accordance with the pertinent rules and guidelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: 
 
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to i) check the completeness of the legal, technical and financial documents during bid opening, bid evaluation and post qualification to ascertain the regularity and propriety of the award of contract and to ensure submission of complete documents pursuant to Sections 9.1 and 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and ii) disqualify participating bidders with incomplete documents/requirement; 
 
BAC to i) ensure that all projects are covered with duly accomplished PPMPs and included in the approved APP prior to commencement of any public bidding as prescribed under Section 7 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; ii) establish a transparent and competitive procurement activity and comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the validity, propriety, and legality of payments; and iii) ensure that a Supplemental/Bid Bulletin is issued at least seven (7) calendar days before the deadline for the submission and receipt of bids whenever the Municipality deems it necessary to clarify or modify any provisions of the Bidding Documents to ensure transparency in the procurement process in compliance with Section 22.5.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and

Municipal Engineer to (i) determine and address the cause of the deficiencies pertaining to the absence of detailed engineering, design plans, design analysis, and technical specifications, and test analysis of construction materials; and (ii) require the maintenance and submission of the Contractor’s daily logbooks for purposes of claiming payment and comply with the requirements of DPWH Department Order No. 26, s. 2000. 
 
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the contractor to immediately post the warranty securities on all projects that will cover the entire defects liability period.
observations.managementAction: The BAC and the Management exerted their best efforts to comply with the requirements of laws, rules and regulations, particularly the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.reason: The submission or attachment of some documents was overlooked by the concerned municipal officials and employees. Hence, this observation is reiterated in the Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to separate the payment for RATA from the monthly salary; and require the necessary documents such as a Certification by the Secretary of the Sanggunian for their attendance to session and a Certification by the Human Resource Management Officer that no government vehicle was used by the respective official to substantiate the claims for RATA.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted the lacking documents required for payment of RATA. The succeeding disbursements for RATA were already supported with the required documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor 	instruct the Property/Supply Officer/Inspector to accomplish the pre-repair inspection report after the conduct of pre-repair inspection of properties subjected for repairs and furnish a copy report to the Audit Team within five (5) days from the date of evaluation/inspection. Likewise, conduct immediate post-repair inspection on the subject properties and submit a report thereon. 
 
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to require the Waste Materials Report as part of the supporting documents in all payments for repairs and maintenance of all government motor vehicles in compliance with Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted the lacking documents required for payment of repairs and maintenance. The succeeding disbursements for repairs and maintenance were already supported with the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the prescribed documentary requirements and ensure that complete supporting documents are attached to the disbursement vouchers before processing and approving payments. We also recommended the strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: The Management exerted their best efforts to comply with the requirements of laws, rules and regulations, particularly Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.reason:
Submission or attachment of some documents was overlooked by the concern municipal officials and employees.

This observation is reiterated in the Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly follow the above provisions of law on the submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) within the specified period to give enough time for the Audit Team to review, monitor, and evaluate the action taken by the management on the audit recommendations.
observations.managementAction: The Management complied with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Solid Waste Management Officer to submit the updated and approved Solid Waste Management Plan to ensure the protection of the public and environment in compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act No. 9003 to the Provincial Solid Waste Management Board, for approval.
observations.managementAction: The Management already took the necessary actions to secure the approval of their Solid Waste Management Plan which was already submitted to the  NSWMC for their approval.
observations.reason: The SWM Plan is already submitted to the NSWMC and still pending approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) instruct the Municipal Engineer and the BAC to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects, to pave the way to their timely implementation and completion; (ii) ensure that the LDC, MPDC, and the Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of project implementation so that necessary interventions/changes may be introduced at the earliest time;   (iii) realign or reprogram the unutilized/unimplemented PPAs identified to priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017; and (iv) conduct intensive review of continuing projects to determine if the PPAs are still part of the vital needs of the Municipality otherwise reprogram them to priority PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The Management exerted their best efforts to fully utilize the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Management cited the lack of interested contractors due to geographical location of the Municipality as one of the reasons for the delayed and/or non-implementation of projects. The utilization of the 20% DF understandably low due to the current situation of COVID19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LDRRM Officer prepare and submit, through the LDRRMC and the LDC, the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRM Fund and other disaster risk reduction and management resources to be certified by the OIC-Municipal Accountant, to the Audit Team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month and copy furnish the OCD. 
 
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the LDRRM Officer, cause the optimum utilization of the LDRRMF and incorporate the unexpended balances in preparing the CY LDRRMFIP to ensure that basic rescue and response equipment are procured in order to enhance the disaster resilience of communities and to institutionalize arrangements and measures for reducing disaster risks.  

Lastly, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and maintain a separate Registry of Appropriation, Allotments and Obligations for LDRRMF for QRF, MOOE and Capital Outlay to facilitate monitoring of its appropriations, expenditures and balances to provide reliable and real-time information for decision making purposes to improve the administration and utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Management already submits the required LDRRMF Utilization Report on a monthly basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Municipal Accountant, exert diligent efforts to enforce the settlement of audit suspensions by sending letters to the concerned persons held responsible/liable requiring the immediate settlement thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted documents for settlement of various Notices of Suspension. Other documents were already prepared by Management and will be submitted to the Audit Team for review and evaluation.
observations.reason: The documents submitted by Management for review and evaluation of the Audit Team cover only a portion of the total amount of Notice of Suspension. There are still transactions that are suspended and the Management were not able to comply with.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct physical count on all inventories on hand periodically which should be reported in the RPCI and shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and (ii) complete the preparation of RPCPPE on the physical count of PPE accounts and submit same to the Audit Team;

instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to fast track the reconciliation of the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records.  In the event that deficiencies/ discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made; and

require the Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which could be undertaken simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: The RPCI and RPCPPE for CY 2020 were submitted to the Audit Team on February 9, 2021.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, the Inventory Committee failed to conduct physical count on some inventory accounts and submit the corresponding RPCI.  In addition, the IIRUP was not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to (a) exert extra efforts to account the unreconciled items of two PNB accounts appearing in the BRS of the LBP accounts to faithfully present the correct balances of the accounts; (b) prepare the necessary adjusting entry to record the unrecorded amount in the Cash in Bank account; and (c) prepare and submit the BRS together with the bank statements, paid checks and debit/credit memos for all the Municipality’s bank accounts within the reglementary period.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor remind the managing head of the depository bank of the Municipality of their duty and responsibility to furnish the Local Accountant with the Bank Statements (BS) including debit/credit memos (DM/CM), paid checks, etc., within five days after the end of each month pursuant to Item 3.1 of COA Circular No. 96-011.
observations.managementAction: The book reconciling item amounting to P768,963.50 in CY 2019 was already adjusted in January 2020.
observations.reason: “Float items” continue to exist in the BRS and submission of BRS was still delayed.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the overdue accounts and render report on the status of the demand letters sent to enforce collection thereof to protect the interest of the government.  
   
We also recommended that local officials expedite the submission of the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs) to the Commission on Audit in line with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, stating the reasons for write-off and extent of follow-up made.  The management should see to it that the request for write - off is duly supported with the documents required in Item 8.3 of the mentioned circular.
observations.managementAction: Receivables from Leticia Mesajon under Advances for Payroll and the accountability of Virginia Gervacio under Other Receivables amounting to P2,720.47 and P2,000.00, respectively were collected in CY 2020.
observations.reason: The long outstanding receivables totaling P2,441,903.18 continued to exist in the books as of December 31, 2020. No request for write-off was received by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The Municipal Treasurer (i) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (ii) instruct the liquidating officer to be more circumspect in the acknowledgement of remittances in terms of accuracy of the data and amount; (iii) instruct all the collecting officers to (1) ensure completeness of information on the face of official receipts and RCD; (2) ensure that each official receipts are properly signed or initialed; (3) properly accomplish the Box C of the RCD; and (4) prepare and submit the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF); (iv) ensure that all the issued and cancelled official receipts are attached to their respective Report of Collections and Deposits; and (v) prepare and submit the monthly Consolidated Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) pursuant to Section 37 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992; and

The Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of the recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the errors in computations, as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Collections for CY 2020 were deposited intact.  The remaining balance of Cash Local Treasury account pertains to collections made at the last working day of the year.
observations.reason: Proper cut-off in the reporting of collections was not yet complied with and errors in recording of collections were still observed.  In addition, the RAAF and CRAAF were not submitted to the Audi Team.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor (a) require strict and uniform implementation of the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to all officials and employees of the municipality; (b) require all accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate all their outstanding cash advances by sending confirmation, notification or demand letters.  For unliquidated cash advances which are non-moving for more than three years, exhaust all remedies to collect or demand to liquidate the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise apply for write off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016 which prescribes the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs); and (c) secure written approval from the Governor for any official travel outside the province in accordance with Section 4 of EO No. 77.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, (a) see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers and liquidation reports are accurate and complete before processing the claims and observe the existing rules and regulations; and (b) reclassify the balance of Advances for Payroll and Advances to Officers and Employees pertaining to the cash advances of accountable officers who were already not in the service, retired or dead totaling P108,182.69.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, claims of some municipal employees were already supported with Certification of Expenses not Requiring Receipts for expenses P300.00 and below.
observations.reason: The balance of Advances to Officers and Employees pertaining to the cash advances of accountable officers who were already not in the service, retired or dead amounting to P108,182.69 were not yet reclassified to Other Receivables account and the unliquidated cash advances to officers and employees totaling P88,095.24 which pertains to various cash advances for travel and supplies granted as early as CY 2008 remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2020.

observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years were already included in the LDRRMFIP and the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF were already prepared and submitted to the Audit Team.  The unexpended LDRRMF was properly transferred and recorded in the Special Trust Fund.
observations.reason: The Municipality incurred lapses in the (a) preparation of its CY 2020 LDRRMFIP; (b) recording and accounting of donation in cash; and (c) procurement, documentation and recording of transactions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to ensure proper compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements.  Meanwhile, cause the investigation on the procurement procedures adopted by the BAC on the subject 12 projects and hold accountable those found remiss of their respective functions and duties.  Also, submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated.  Moreover, furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution thereof as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009.  

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are valid, proper and legal and supported with complete documentation before processing the claims in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160.  Submit also the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, review of the submitted contracts disclosed that the notices issued to the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid were already attached to the contract documents and some of the lapses noted in the documentation were already addressed and corrected.
observations.reason: Minor lapses and deficiencies in the procurement of infrastructure projects as well as on the documents attached to the payments of progress billings were still noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  instruct the BAC to: (a) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five working days from the execution of the contract as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; (b) make sure that Purchase Orders contain basic data/information as prescribed by COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996; (c) observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; (d) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post-Qualification Evaluation Reports; (e) submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated; and (f) prepare various reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors which could mislead the readers/users of the reports.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, (a) see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are complete before processing the claims in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b) be guided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 for proper recording of transactions in accordance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS).
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, some contracts/ purchase orders were already submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period and some lapses noted were already addressed.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, the brand name of the procured vehicle was still not indicated in the contract/purchase order and minor lapses/ deficiencies in the procurement of goods as well as on the documents attached to the payments were still noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:

Municipal Accountant and the concerned employee who prepares the payroll to strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 103.  The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, applications for leave of the department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due;

Municipal Accountant (i) make sure that required supporting documents of payrolls are complete pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445; and (ii) take extra care in recording of transactions in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and

Human Resource Officer to scrutinize the Applications for Leave of each officials and employees and make sure that they are properly filled up to avoid confusion as to the type of leave.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the refund of excess payments of RATA amounting to P90,750.00 to officials and employees of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, payments of RATA were already recorded in the proper account.  In addition, the attached Applications for Leave (AFL) were already properly filled-up as to the types of leaves.
observations.reason: Members of the sanggunian and department heads were still paid in full despite of absences resulting in excess payment of P39,750.00.  Moreover, some of the supporting documents to payrolls such as the Daily Time Records, AFL and travel orders were still not attached.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Municipal Local School Board (LSB) to: (i) adhere to the provisions of Section 272 of R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 in planning the programs, projects and activities (PPAs) to be charged to the fund and its proper utilization; (ii) review and approve the SEF budget for the ensuing year on the prescribed schedule as stated in the said joint circular; and (iii) make sure that PPAs included in the budget are properly supported with details of the PPAs to avoid charging of expenses which are not related to the purpose of the fund; and

instruct the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, to see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers/liquidation reports are complete before processing the claim in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2017-001.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, expenditures charged to the fund were already in accordance with the purpose of the fund and the lapses noted in the documentation of transactions were already addressed.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, the Municipal Local School Board (LSB) failed anew to observe the prescribed schedule of review and approval of the Special Education Fund (SEF) budget, thereby limiting the Provincial LSB to consider augmentation to municipalities in the review of the provincial SEF Budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the approving officials to refrain from paying terminal leave benefits of officials and employees who are not yet resigned, retired or separated from service.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the legality and propriety of the payments made as well as the completeness of supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, no more terminal leave benefits were paid to the elective officials who are not yet separated from the service.  Some of the payments of terminal leave benefits were already supported with complete documentation.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, some documents were still not attached to the disbursement vouchers.  Also, it was noted again that the applications for leave were not properly accomplished as the portion 6.d were not checked if commutation is requested or not and the purpose of the Clearance was not specified in some of the claims.  AOM No. 2021-006(5) dated March 8, 2021 was issued.

observations.managementAction: In CY 2020, non-moving/unimplemented projects of prior years were reprogrammed to other development projects.  Some of the lapses noted in the documentation of transactions were already addressed.
observations.reason: The Municipality (a) failed to implement 10 projects funded under the current and continuing appropriations of the 20% Development Fund (DF) with a total budget amount of P21,040,043.44; (b) charged expenses which do not partake the nature of capital expenditures; and (c) incurred deficiencies and lapses in the documentation of transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (i) Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the APP is consistent with the Annual Budget, reflects the entire procurement activity of the agency for the calendar year and contain all necessary information required under GPPB Circular No. 1-2009 dated 20, January 2009; and (ii) Department Heads to prepare their respective PPMP for their different programs, activities and projects pursuant to Section 7.3.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to facilitate the preparation of a compliant APP.
observations.reason:
The APP for CY 2019 did not contain the details required by the GPPB Circular and the same was not posted in the GPPB website.

This observation is reiterated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the overdue accounts and render report on the status of the demand letters sent to enforce collection thereof to protect the interest of the government. Section IV. General Guidelines of COA Memorandum No. 2017-010 dated May 15, 2017 provides that: “x x x. The Audit Team Leader (ATL) shall request from the Accountant proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect receivables and liquidate cash advances and fund transfers as basis for the issuance of the final demand letter. There is therefore a need for the Accountant to issue demand letter to the AO/employee/recipient/NGOs to liquidate their due and demandable cash advances/fund transfers.” 
   
We also recommended that local officials expedite the submission of the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers of National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs) to the Commission on Audit in line with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, stating the reasons for write-off and extent of follow-up made.  The management should see to it that the request for write – off is duly supported with the documents required in Item 8.3 of the mentioned circular.
observations.reason: The long outstanding receivables totaling P2,446,623.65 continued to exist in the books as of December 31, 2019. No request for write-off was received by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, MSWDO and the MAO to exert their best effort to collect long-outstanding receivables by (i) continuously sending collection notice to concerned debtors; and (ii) instilling in the minds of future beneficiaries the need to return the amount to ensure fund availability and continuity of the program.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (a) BAC and the Municipal Engineer to promptly implement the projects in accordance with the specific purpose of the trust fund for the constituents to enjoy the benefits of the government’s programs and projects at the soonest time; (b) Municipal Agricultural Officer to coordinate with the Fishery Chairman of Looc for the prompt submission of the list of qualified fisher folks to facilitate the release/distribution of incentives amounting to P1,669,521.80; and (c) Municipal Treasurer to remit the unused balance of Due to NGAs account to the concerned source agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to i) check the completeness of the legal, technical and financial documents during bid opening, bid evaluation and post-qualification to ascertain the regularity and propriety of the award of contract and to ensure submission of complete documents pursuant to Sections 9.1 and 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and                     ii) disqualify participating bidders with incomplete documents/requirement;
BAC to i) ensure that all projects are covered with duly accomplished PPMPs and included in the approved APP prior to commencement of any public bidding as prescribed under Section 7 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; ii) establish a transparent and competitive procurement activity and comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the validity, propriety, and legality of payments; and iii) ensure that a Supplemental/Bid Bulletin is issued at least seven (7) calendar days before the deadline for the submission and receipt of bids whenever the Municipality deems it necessary to clarify or modify any provisions of the Bidding Documents to ensure transparency in the procurement process in compliance with Section 22.5.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Municipal Engineer to (i) determine and address the cause of the deficiencies pertaining to the absence of detailed engineering, design plans, design analysis, and technical specifications, and test analysis of construction materials; and (ii) require the maintenance and submission of the Contractor’s daily logbooks for purposes of claiming payment and comply with the requirements of DPWH Department Order No. 26, s. 2000.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the contractor to immediately post the warranty securities on all projects that will cover the entire defects liability period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to separate the payment for RATA from the monthly salary; and require the necessary documents such as a Certification by the Secretary of the Sanggunian for their attendance to session and a Certification by the Human Resource Management Officer that no government vehicle was used by the respective official to substantiate the claims for RATA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Property/Supply Officer/Inspector to accomplish the pre-repair inspection report after the conduct of pre-repair inspection of properties subjected for repairs and furnish a copy report to the Audit Team within five (5) days from the date of evaluation/inspection. Likewise, conduct immediate post-repair inspection on the subject properties and submit a report thereon.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to require the Waste Materials Report as part of the supporting documents in all payments for repairs and maintenance of all government motor vehicles in compliance with Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the prescribed documentary requirements and ensure that complete supporting documents are attached to the disbursement vouchers before processing and approving payments. We also recommended the strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Management strictly follow the above provisions of law on the submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) within the specified period to give enough time for the Audit Team to review, monitor, and evaluate the action taken by the management on the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Solid Waste Management Officer to submit the updated and approved Solid Waste Management Plan to ensure the protection of the public and environment in compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act No. 9003 to the Provincial Solid Waste Management Board, for approval.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through the GAD focal point system (i) synchronize the preparation of GAD Plan and Budget with the planning and budgeting calendar to enable its timely submission to the PPDO to check its alignment with the priorities of the Province and then to the DILG Provincial office for review and endorsement; (ii) develop PPAs that would address gender issues in the Municipality to strengthen its gender-responsive governance; and (iii) ensure that GAD Fund is fully utilized efficiently and effectively in compliance with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 as amended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) instruct the Municipal Engineer and the BAC to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects, to pave the way to their timely implementation and completion; (ii) ensure that the LDC, MPDC, and the Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of project implementation so that necessary interventions/changes may be introduced at the earliest time;   (iii) realign or reprogram the unutilized/unimplemented PPAs identified to priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017; and (iv) conduct intensive review of continuing projects to determine if the PPAs are still part of the vital needs of the Municipality otherwise reprogram them to priority PPAs.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer prepare and submit, through the LDRRMC and the LDC, the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRM Fund and other disaster risk reduction and management resources to be certified by the OIC – Municipal Accountant, to the Audit Team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month and copy furnish the OCD.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the LDRRM Officer, cause the optimum utilization of the LDRRMF and incorporate the unexpended balances in preparing the CY LDRRMFIP to ensure that basic rescue and response equipment are procured in order to enhance the disaster resilience of communities and to institutionalize arrangements and measures for reducing disaster risks.
Lastly, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments, and Obligations for LDRRMF for QRF, MOOE and Capital Outlay to facilitate monitoring of its appropriations, expenditures and balances to provide reliable and real-time information for decision making purposes to improve the administration and utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Municipal Accountant, exert diligent efforts to enforce the settlement of audit suspensions by sending letters to the concerned persons held responsible/liable requiring the immediate settlement thereof.

     a. require the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct  physical  count  on

        the RPCI and shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not  later
        than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first  and  second

        Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which could be  undertaken
        simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and  eventual

        Management gave no comment on our observations and recommendations.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that  the  Municipal  Mayor
   instruct the BAC to: (a) furnish the  Audit  Team  with  a  copy  of  the
   Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement  and  its  attachments  within  five

   (d) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-

   reports.

        The Municipal Accountant promised that for subsequent transactions,
   supporting documents attached to the vouchers are  valid,  proper,  legal

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, MSWDO and the MAO to exert their best effort to collect long-outstanding receivables by (i) sending collection notice to concerned debtors; and (ii) instilling in the minds of future beneficiaries the need to return the amount to ensure fund availability and continuity of the program.

observations.managementAction: Management sent collection notices to the concerned debtors.
observations.reason: There are still uncollected loans from previous years despite the collection notice. The Management devised another payment scheme for the borrowers. Every time that a borrower with past due account renew his business licence or transact any affairs with the Municipality of Looc, the said borrower is required to pay the amount of his outstanding loans with the Municipality before he can proceed with his other concerns. The down side of this payment scheme is that, not all borrowers might have transactions with the Municipality, hence not all borrowers with past due accounts may pay the amount due thereon. Based on the schedule provided in the Annex of the same observation in this AAR, we can draw a conclusion that there are many borrowers that did not voluntarily pay their obligations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Solid Waste Management Officer to submit the approved updated Solid Waste Management Plan in compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act No. 9003 to the Provincial Solid Waste Management Board, for approval.
observations.managementAction: Management is in the process of revising their 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (a) officers concerned to promptly implement the projects in accordance with the specific purpose of the trust fund in order that the constituents could enjoy the benefits of the government’s programs and projects at earliest time; (b) Municipal Accountant to submit liquidation reports to the DA and DSWD pertaining to the collections of the Cash Assistance for Livelihood Program totaling P714,804.00; (c) Municipal Treasurer to remit collections amounting to P714,804.00 to the concerned source agencies; and (d) Municipal Agricultural Officer to coordinate with the Fishery Chairman in Looc for the submission of the list of qualified fisherfolks to facilitate the release/distribution of incentives amounting P1,728,430.90.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented their priority projects particularly those related to water supply system the benefits of which will be enjoyed by the constituents. The counterpart from the 20% Development Fund was used for the implementation of their priority development projects.
observations.reason:
The unexpended fund balance from the NGAs, particularly the DILG-BUB fund for Water Program, is still unremitted to source agency since the management of LGU-Looc filed a pending action against the erring contractor of the BUB Water program project. The Management of LGU-Looc also sent a letter to the DILG requesting that the unused funds be used for other related projects of the Municipality, citing a specific provision in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) that the funds will only be remitted back in full to the DILG if the LGU did not take any action to implement the project, arguing that the LGU did take action for the implementation of the project which, unfortunately, the contractor failed to deliver, thus, the said request to the DILG. 
The LGU is currently waiting for the response from the DILG to their letter request.

This observation is discussed in detail in Part II of this AAR.

The BAC to (a) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract/Purchase Order and their attachments within five (5) working days from the execution/issuance as required under Sections 3.1.1 and 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001; (b) observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; (c) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post- Qualification Evaluation Reports; (d) submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the same IRR and address the deficiencies enumerated; and (e) prepare the required reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors which could mislead readers and users of the reports; and

The Municipal Accountant (a) be guided with the requirements of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160  for proper documentation of every claim against government funds; and (b) submit the lacking documents mentioned above.

observations.recommendation:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of
the physical count with the accounting records and submit the

Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections

certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and
